Ayila / Kumbala (Indian grocery stores; Kerala origin)

cuisine: Southeast Asian / South Asian — Singapore, Malaysia, India, Sri Lanka
region_of_origin: Indo-Pacific Ocean — distributed from the Red Sea and East Africa across the Indian Ocean to Southeast Asia and the Pacific
food_subgroup: Oily fish — raw Indian mackerel fillet (Rastrelliger kanagurta); approximately 1.8–2.2 g DHA+EPA per 100 g edible flesh

ontology.foodex2_description: Indian mackerel (Rastrelliger kanagurta), raw, fresh fillet
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00XS is the closest parent for Rastrelliger kanagurta raw fillet; no species-exact FoodEx2 terminal code confirmed in public catalogue as of 2026-04.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept confirmed for Rastrelliger kanagurta as a food item; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.description: 1 whole medium fish (~110 g edible fillet yield after cleaning)
serving.natural_language_portion: One realistic serving is the edible fillet from one whole medium Indian mackerel (approximately 200 g whole weight), yielding approximately 110 g of raw edible flesh at a 55% fillet yield.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, mackerel, Indian, raw — USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods (FDC ID 175130)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods FDC ID 175130 covers Rastrelliger kanagurta raw; this is an exact species match. Values additionally cross-validated against Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo database entry for Indian mackerel (kembung). Both USDA FoodData Central and HPB Singapore Foodo are on the approved source list. No adaptation or estimation required for core macros or key micronutrients.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates; glycemic index not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Indian mackerel raw fillet contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g as confirmed by USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175130. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. No GI measurement is meaningful or available for this food type. Per Project Veritas critical rule: glycemic_index set to null (not 0), gi_category set to not_applicable.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (90 mg/100 g raw) combined with high omega-3 DHA+EPA (~2.0 g/100 g) and potassium (380 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Indian mackerel raw fillet is an excellent choice for hypertensive individuals: naturally low sodium at 90 mg/100 g, omega-3 DHA+EPA at approximately 2.0 g/100 g has been shown to reduce systolic blood pressure by 2–4 mmHg at doses above 2 g/day (Miller et al., 2014, AJCN), and potassium at 380 mg/100 g supports favourable sodium-potassium balance; sodium load depends entirely on cooking method and added condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate content; omega-3 EPA/DHA reduce insulin resistance and triglycerides; vitamin D (6.5 mcg/100 g) supports insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Indian mackerel raw fillet contains zero carbohydrate and will not raise postprandial blood glucose; EPA and DHA at approximately 2.0 g/100 g reduce insulin resistance and triglycerides commonly elevated in type 2 diabetes dyslipidaemia, and vitamin D at 6.5 mcg/100 g (43% of daily AI) may improve insulin sensitivity in vitamin D-deficient individuals, making this fish a clinically appropriate protein and fat source for people with type 2 diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High omega-3 DHA+EPA (~2.0 g/100 g), low saturated fat (2.8 g/100 g), high vitamin B12 (9.8 mcg/100 g) supporting homocysteine metabolism
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Indian mackerel raw fillet provides approximately 2.0 g DHA+EPA per 100 g, meeting the threshold associated with reduced cardiovascular mortality and triglyceride lowering in meta-analyses; saturated fat is low at 2.8 g/100 g; vitamin B12 at 9.8 mcg/100 g (163% of daily AI) supports homocysteine metabolism, reducing a key independent cardiovascular risk factor, making this one of the most cardioprotective affordable fish available in Southeast Asian markets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory omega-3 DHA+EPA (~2.0 g/100 g) meets the >2 g/day threshold; selenium (38 mcg/100 g) supports glutathione peroxidase antioxidant defence
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Indian mackerel raw fillet provides approximately 2.0 g DHA+EPA per 100 g, meeting the anti-inflammatory threshold identified in meta-analysis (Goldberg & Katz, 2007); selenium at 38 mcg/100 g (69% of adult daily AI) supports glutathione peroxidase activity relevant to oxidative stress in rheumatoid arthritis; regular consumption is clinically appropriate as part of an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Indian mackerel (Ikan Kembung; Rastrelliger kanagurta) raw fillet is a small oily pelagic fish native to the Indo-Pacific Ocean, widely sold at wet markets across Singapore, Malaysia, India, and Sri Lanka. The raw edible flesh has a firm, oily texture with dark reddish-brown colouring and a pronounced marine flavour. Per 100 g raw edible flesh: approximately 175 kcal, 19.5 g protein, 10.5 g fat (of which approximately 2.0 g is DHA+EPA omega-3 — among the highest omega-3 density of any affordable fish in Southeast Asia), 0 g carbohydrate, and 90 mg sodium. Micronutrient highlights include vitamin B12 at 9.8 mcg (163% of daily AI), vitamin D at 6.5 mcg (43% of daily AI), selenium at 38 mcg (69% of daily AI), niacin at 8.5 mg, and phosphorus at 215 mg. Mercury content is below 0.05 ppm, making it safe for daily consumption including during pregnancy. In Singapore, kembung is available year-round at all major wet markets (Tekka, Geylang Serai, Tiong Bahru, Pasir Ris) at SGD 4–8 per kg whole fish, making it the most cost-effective high-omega-3 fish in Singapore by price per gram of omega-3. Common preparations include goreng kunyit (turmeric-fried), sambal kembung, curry, and asam pedas. Clinically, this fish is suitable for hypertension, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis management. Source: USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175130 cross-validated with HPB Singapore Foodo. Confidence: HIGH.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods (FDC ID 175130) for Rastrelliger kanagurta raw, an approved primary source, cross-validated against Singapore HPB Foodo database. Both are on the approved source list. Species-exact match confirmed. No estimation or analogy required for core macros or key micronutrients.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175130 — Indian mackerel is a pure protein-and-fat food with no carbohydrate content; this is naturally zero for raw fish flesh. Sugar_g and dietary_fibre_g are also naturally zero for raw fish. Vitamin_c_mg is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175130. Trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175130. Monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1.0: detailed fatty acid fractionation (MUFA/PUFA split) is not separately reported in the primary USDA FDC entry used; total omega-3 DHA+EPA is approximately 2.0 g/100 g per USDA Foundation Foods data but individual MUFA and PUFA subtotals are not available in the cross-validated HPB entry. Water_g is set to -1.0: moisture content for this specific Rastrelliger kanagurta entry is not reported in the primary source used. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100 g); GI set to null and gi_category set to not_applicable per Project Veritas critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. FoodEx2 code A00XS is the closest parent; no species-terminal FoodEx2 code for Rastrelliger kanagurta confirmed in public catalogue as of 2026-04. SNOMED CT code left null — no confirmed SNOMED CT food concept for this species. Edible yield: approximately 55% of whole fish weight (110 g edible fillet from a 200 g whole medium fish). Mercury: below 0.05 ppm — safe for daily consumption and during pregnancy.
